sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 9, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE on green object': 'Moves selection (dark gray ring) to clicked green object; previous selection gets gray marker VERIFIED(step 4)', 'RIGHT': 'No visible effect VERIFIED(step 3)', 'ACTION7': "Execution failed with 'Unknown action' error (step 5)", 'MOUSE sequence through all 5 green objects': 'Board resets to original state after all 5 selected (step 6-7)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'N': 'light green plus-shaped objects (12 pixels each, 5 total - targets to select)', 'G': 'dark gray selection ring on currently selected target', 'g': 'gray marker on previously selected objects', 'w': 'light gray 4x4 grid cells (16 pixels each)', 'B': 'black L-shaped border separating two regions', 'b': 'blue thin accent borders', 'W': 'white inner background', 'S': 'sky blue outer background'}
- Win pattern: Unknown - selecting all 5 green objects resets the board rather than completing the level
- Cross-level notes: Level 1: two grid regions (top-left 3x7, bottom-right 6x3) separated by black L-border. 5 green plus objects total.
Current level:
- World model: Level 1. Two grid regions with 5 green plus objects. Clicking selects them one at a time with selection ring and gray markers. After selecting all 5, board resets.
- Goal model: Unknown - selecting all 5 green objects resets board. Need to find correct interaction pattern.
- Recent findings: ['Board resets after clicking all 5 green objects in sequence', 'No selection ring or markers remain after reset']
- Open questions: ['What is the actual goal?', 'Is there a specific order to click objects?', 'Do directional keys do anything with selection?', 'What does ACTION7 actually do?']
- Plan: Try clicking green objects in a different order, or try using directional keys with an active selection
- Strategy log: ['Clicked all 5 green objects sequentially: board reset instead of completing level']
- Failed probes: ['RIGHT with selection active: no effect (step 3)', 'ACTION7: execution error (step 5)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 8 of about 32 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
RIGHT didn't change anything again. Let me check what actually changed - maybe there's a subtle HUD change I'm missing. But more importantly, I need to think about what the actual goal could be.
Looking at the board again:
- Two grid regions separated by a black L-shaped border
- 5 green plus objects scattered across the grids
- The grids have light gray cells
Maybe the goal isn't about selecting the green objects but about something else entirely. Let me think about what ACTION7 could be - it's listed as a valid action but failed when I tried it. Maybe it needs to be called differently.
Wait, let me reconsider. The valid_actions include ACTION7 which is unusual. In ARC games, special actions like this often represent a "submit" or "confirm" action. But it failed with "Unknown action" error.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe I need to click on the gray cells or the borders, not the green objects. Or maybe I need to click on empty cells within the grids.
Actually, let me look more carefully at the image. The grid has cells that are light gray (w). Some cells contain green plus objects (N). The black border (B) separates the two regions. 
Wait - maybe the goal is to fill in the grid cells with the green objects? Or maybe I need to click on empty cells to place something?
Let me try clicking on an empty gray cell to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# RIGHT still no effect. Let me try clicking on an empty gray cell instead
# First check what changed
before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after_seg = current_frame.segmentation

G_before = [n for n in before_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'G']
G_after = [n for n in after_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'G']
print(f"G before: {len(G_before)}, after: {len(G_after)}")

# Try clicking on an empty cell in the grid
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 22}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE on green object": "Moves selection to that green object; previous selection gets gray marker VERIFIED(step 4)", "RIGHT": "No visible effect even with selection VERIFIED(step 9)", "ACTION7": "Execution failed (step 5)", "Select all 5 then reset": "Board resets after all 5 selected VERIFIED(step 7)"}, "object_taxonomy": {"N": "light green plus-shaped objects (targets)", "G": "dark gray selection ring", "g": "gray marker on previously selected", "w": "light gray grid cells", "B": "black L-border", "b": "blue accent", "W": "white bg", "S": "sky blue bg"}, "hud_map": {}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Level 1. Two grid regions with 5 green plus objects. Clicking selects them sequentially. Board resets after all 5 selected. Directional keys have no effect.", "goal_model": "Unknown - need to try different interaction patterns", "recent_findings": ["RIGHT has no effect even with active selection"], "open_questions": ["What does clicking empty cells do?", "What is the actual goal?"], "current_plan": "Try clicking empty gray cell to see what happens", "strategy_log": ["Clicked all 5 sequentially: board reset", "RIGHT with selection: no effect"], "failed_probes": ["RIGHT: no effect", "ACTION7: error", "Select all 5: reset"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
